h1Title: Miliary Tuberculosis: Symptoms, Causes, Diagnosis, and Treatment
heroOverview: Miliary tuberculosis is a severe, disseminated form of tuberculosis in which Mycobacterium tuberculosis bacilli spread hematogenously from a primary focus and seed every organ simultaneously, producing innumerable 1-2 mm granulomas that resemble millet seeds on imaging — the origin of the name. It accounts for approximately 1-2% of all tuberculosis cases globally, but carries case fatality rates of 25-30% even with treatment and up to 80-100% if untreated. The WHO estimates that 10 million people develop active tuberculosis each year; miliary disease represents the fulminant end of that spectrum, occurring most often when immune defenses are severely impaired — typically in HIV-infected individuals, immunosuppressed patients, malnourished children, or the elderly. Standard four-drug anti-TB therapy (RIPE) initiated promptly is life-saving.

keyStats.prevalence: Miliary TB accounts for approximately 1-2% of all active TB cases and 20% of all extrapulmonary TB in reported series; estimated 100,000-200,000 cases per year globally
keyStats.demographics: Bimodal age distribution: children under 5 and adults with HIV infection, organ transplant, anti-TNF therapy, or advanced malnutrition carry highest risk; more common in sub-Saharan Africa, South Asia, and Eastern Europe
keyStats.avgAge: Children under 5: hematogenous dissemination within 6 months of primary infection without BCG vaccination; adults: typically age 30-60 with an identified immunosuppressive risk factor
keyStats.globalCases: WHO Global TB Report 2023: 1.3 million TB deaths in 2022; miliary/disseminated TB contributes approximately 10-15% of TB mortality worldwide

definition: Miliary tuberculosis (ICD-10: A19.2) is a form of active tuberculosis arising from hematogenous dissemination of Mycobacterium tuberculosis, resulting in the formation of granulomas in multiple organs simultaneously. The name derives from the Latin word for millet (milium), reflecting the characteristic 1-2 mm scattered nodules seen on chest radiographs and CT scans. Pathophysiology: a primary or reactivated TB focus erodes into a blood vessel or the thoracic duct, releasing bacilli into the systemic or pulmonary circulation. Dissemination seeds the lungs, liver, spleen, bone marrow, kidneys, meninges, adrenal glands, and eyes with equal bacillary load. Each seeded organ develops a granulomatous response: central caseous necrosis surrounded by epithelioid macrophages, Langhans giant cells, and lymphocytes. In immunocompromised individuals, granuloma formation is impaired and a non-reactive or cryptic miliary pattern develops with rapid clinical deterioration. The two forms recognized clinically are: classic miliary TB (febrile constitutional illness with the miliary chest radiographic pattern) and cryptic miliary TB (insidious course with minimal radiographic findings, often diagnosed post-mortem or by bone marrow biopsy). It is managed by infectious disease, pulmonary medicine, and critical care specialists; all active TB must be reported to public health authorities.

typesClassification.description: Acute or subacute onset with high fever, constitutional symptoms, and the characteristic bilateral miliary nodular pattern on chest X-ray or CT (1-2 mm discrete nodules in all lung zones). Most common in immunocompetent children and adults.

Insidious onset in elderly or profoundly immunosuppressed patients (advanced HIV, transplant recipients) with minimal or absent chest radiographic changes. Diagnosis often delayed until bone marrow or liver biopsy is performed. High mortality from delayed recognition.

Rare, fulminant form with overwhelming bacterial dissemination and absent granuloma formation due to deficient cellular immunity. The histological appearance shows sheets of macrophages laden with acid-fast bacilli without well-formed granulomas. Nearly universally fatal without rapid diagnosis.
Concurrent CNS seeding occurs in 20-30% of miliary TB cases. Carries higher mortality than miliary TB alone and requires prolonged treatment (12 months minimum) with corticosteroids.

HIV co-infected patients account for 40-60% of miliary TB in high-burden countries. Chest X-ray findings may be atypical or absent; extrapulmonary seeding (liver, spleen, marrow, adrenal) is more prominent. Must balance antiretroviral and anti-TB therapy timing to avoid immune reconstitution inflammatory syndrome (IRIS).

primarySymptoms: Prolonged fever (typically 38-40°C) persisting for 2-6 weeks without an obvious focus, often the dominant presenting complaint and the reason medical attention is first sought.
Severe progressive fatigue, anorexia, and weight loss (typically 5-10 kg over weeks to months) from systemic mycobacterial disease and cytokine-driven cachexia.
Dry cough and progressive exertional dyspnea as the miliary lung nodules coalesce and impair gas exchange; frank respiratory failure can supervene.
Night sweats — drenching nocturnal perspiration that soaks clothing and bedding — one of the classic constitutional B-symptoms of tuberculosis.
Hepatosplenomegaly detected on physical examination in 50-70% of miliary TB patients, reflecting granulomatous infiltration of the liver and spleen.

Lymphadenopathy — generalized or regional — from granulomatous involvement of lymph nodes; lymph nodes may be soft and non-tender in miliary disease unlike the firm, matted nodes of classic TB lymphadenitis.

Choroidal tubercles — yellow-white retinal lesions visible on fundoscopic examination in 10-30% of miliary TB patients; their presence is virtually pathognomonic and confirms dissemination without requiring biopsy.
Headache, neck stiffness, photophobia, and confusion when concurrent meningeal involvement develops — present in approximately 20-30% of miliary TB cases.
Bone marrow involvement causing pancytopenia (anemia, thrombocytopenia, leukopenia) visible on blood count in up to 50% of miliary TB cases.
earlyWarningSigns: Prolonged unexplained fever (more than 2 weeks) with significant weight loss, night sweats, and no alternative diagnosis in a patient from or with exposure to a TB-endemic region.

New-onset exertional dyspnea out of proportion to the apparent degree of chest involvement in an immunosuppressed patient — miliary lung disease reduces diffusing capacity before radiographic changes become apparent.

Unexplained pancytopenia (low hemoglobin, low platelets, low white cells simultaneously) on a blood count — bone marrow granulomatous involvement is an early miliary TB manifestation that can precede respiratory symptoms.
Hyponatremia (low sodium) with fever in a patient with known or suspected TB history — adrenal granulomas causing sub-clinical adrenal insufficiency are a recognized miliary TB complication.
emergencySigns: Acute respiratory failure with bilateral miliary infiltrates and hypoxia (SpO2 below 90% on room air) — indicates diffuse miliary lung involvement requiring ICU-level respiratory support.
Altered consciousness, seizures, or focal neurological deficits in a patient with miliary TB — meningeal or cerebral involvement requiring immediate lumbar puncture and corticosteroid addition.

Refractory hypotension in miliary TB — may reflect Waterhouse-Friderichsen-like bilateral adrenal hemorrhage or overwhelming sepsis from miliary dissemination; empirical hydrocortisone and cultures are indicated.
Thrombocytopenia with purpura and active bleeding in miliary TB — bone marrow granuloma-driven thrombocytopenia can be severe; platelet transfusion and urgent TB treatment are required.

causes.cause: Hematogenous spread from a primary pulmonary focus
causes.description: In children, primary TB infection in the lung may progress to hematogenous dissemination within 6 months when cellular immunity is immature, particularly without BCG vaccination. The primary complex (lung focus plus hilar lymph node) erodes into a pulmonary vein or thoracic duct, releasing bacilli into the systemic circulation.

causes.cause: Reactivation of a latent TB focus with vascular erosion
causes.description: Decades-old latent TB foci in the lung, kidney, bone, or lymph nodes can reactivate under immunosuppressive conditions (HIV progression, anti-TNF therapy, organ transplant, malignancy, malnutrition) and, if adjacent to a blood vessel, seed the circulation. This is the dominant mechanism in adult miliary TB.

HIV reduces CD4+ T-lymphocyte counts, critically impairing the granuloma formation that contains Mycobacterium tuberculosis. When CD4 counts fall below 200 cells/µL, disseminated miliary TB becomes substantially more likely; approximately 40-60% of miliary TB cases in high-burden countries occur in HIV-infected individuals.

causes.cause: Anti-TNF-alpha therapy and other biologic immunosuppressants
causes.description: Tumor necrosis factor-alpha is essential for granuloma integrity. Inhibition by drugs such as infliximab, adalimumab, or etanercept for rheumatoid arthritis, Crohn's disease, or psoriasis dramatically increases the risk of TB reactivation, including miliary dissemination. Screening for latent TB before anti-TNF initiation is mandated by NICE, ACR, and EULAR guidelines.

Severe protein-energy malnutrition impairs macrophage phagocytic function and lymphocyte proliferation, creating the immunological conditions for dissemination. Children with kwashiorkor or marasmus in TB-endemic settings have significantly higher rates of miliary progression than well-nourished children with primary infection.

causes.cause: Organ transplantation and chronic corticosteroid therapy
causes.description: Solid-organ and hematopoietic stem-cell transplant recipients have 40-70-fold higher incidence of active TB than the general population; miliary disease can arise from donor-derived TB, reactivation of recipient latent TB, or de novo primary infection. Long-term corticosteroid use (prednisone equivalent above 15 mg/day for more than 4 weeks) is an independent miliary TB risk factor.

riskFactors.factor: HIV infection with CD4 count below 200 cells/µL
riskFactors.description: The dominant individual risk factor for miliary TB globally; antiretroviral therapy and TB preventive therapy (isoniazid or rifapentine) significantly reduce risk. CD4 count restoration above 200 cells/µL reduces dissemination probability substantially.

riskFactors.factor: Age under 5 years without BCG vaccination
riskFactors.description: Children under 5 have an immature cellular immune response and a 20-40% risk of miliary dissemination within 6 months of primary TB infection without BCG vaccination, versus less than 5% in BCG-vaccinated children.

TNF-alpha blockade increases active TB risk 4-25 fold; miliary and extrapulmonary forms are over-represented. LTBI screening and treatment before initiation is standard of care per ACR, EULAR, and NICE guidelines.

Transplant-related immunosuppression (calcineurin inhibitors, mycophenolate, steroids) creates reactivation risk from latent TB harbored by donor or recipient; screening both donor and recipient for LTBI before transplant is recommended.

riskFactors.factor: Malnutrition (BMI below 18.5 or severe protein deficiency)
riskFactors.description: Underweight individuals have 2-3× higher active TB incidence than well-nourished individuals; severe malnutrition impairs granuloma formation and increases dissemination risk. Nutritional supplementation is a component of WHO TB control strategy.

riskFactors.factor: Born in or travel from a high TB-burden country
riskFactors.description: WHO lists 30 high-burden countries (India, Indonesia, China, Philippines, Pakistan, Nigeria, South Africa, Bangladesh, DRC, Ethiopia) accounting for 87% of all active TB. Latent infection acquired in these countries can reactivate decades later in immunosuppressed individuals in low-burden countries.

riskFactors.factor: Silicosis, diabetes mellitus, or chronic kidney disease
riskFactors.description: Silicosis increases TB risk 6-30 fold; diabetes mellitus doubles TB incidence and progression risk; end-stage renal disease triples it. These comorbidities impair macrophage killing capacity and lymphocyte function.

affectedDemographics: Sub-Saharan Africa and South Asia (India, Indonesia, Pakistan, Bangladesh): the highest-burden regions; HIV-TB co-infection drives miliary TB in sub-Saharan Africa where 40-60% of miliary cases are HIV-positive.
Children under 5 in TB-endemic countries without BCG vaccination: miliary TB is the most feared early complication of primary TB infection in this age group.
Immunocompromised adults in high-income countries on anti-TNF therapy or post-transplant immunosuppression who have latent TB from prior endemic exposure.
Homeless individuals, people who inject drugs, and incarcerated populations have 10-50× higher TB incidence than the general population; disseminated forms are over-represented.
Elderly patients in long-term care facilities in low-incidence countries can reactivate TB acquired decades earlier, sometimes presenting as cryptic miliary disease with diagnostic delay.
diagnosisOverview: Miliary tuberculosis is one of medicine's great diagnostic challenges because its presentation overlaps with fever of unknown origin, lymphoma, disseminated fungal infection, and sarcoidosis. Diagnosis rests on combining clinical suspicion (immunosuppression, endemic exposure, B-symptoms, choroidal tubercles) with imaging and microbiological confirmation. The classic chest radiograph shows bilateral, symmetrical, uniformly sized 1-2 mm nodules distributed throughout all lung zones — the miliary pattern — present in about 75-85% of cases; CT of the thorax is more sensitive, detecting miliary nodules 1-2 weeks before they appear on plain X-ray. High-resolution CT (HRCT) of the chest with diffuse micro-nodules in a random distribution, tree-in-bud pattern, and interlobular septal thickening is highly characteristic. Microbiological confirmation uses sputum smear and culture (positive in 30-50% of miliary TB) and induced sputum when spontaneous sputum is unavailable; bronchoscopy with BAL increases yield to 70-90%. Blood cultures for mycobacteria (Bactec mycobacterial blood culture bottles) are positive in 20-50% of HIV-co-infected miliary TB. Bone marrow biopsy is the highest-yield single biopsy procedure (70-90% positivity) in cases without sputum and is particularly valuable in cryptic miliary TB with pancytopenia. Liver biopsy, trans-bronchial biopsy, and lymph node biopsy show granulomas with caseous necrosis in 50-80% of cases. Fundoscopy for choroidal tubercles should be performed in all suspected cases. Serum LDH and ALP are typically elevated. Lumbar puncture must be performed if any neurological signs are present. Interferon-gamma release assays (IGRAs) and TST are positive in only 50-70% of miliary TB (lower in HIV-infected individuals) and their negativity does not exclude diagnosis. Nucleic acid amplification tests (Xpert MTB/RIF) on sputum, BAL, or CSF return results within 2 hours and simultaneously detect rifampicin resistance.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Most sensitive imaging test; detects bilateral 1-2 mm random miliary nodules in all lung zones 1-2 weeks before chest X-ray; also identifies mediastinal adenopathy, pleural effusions, and complications
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: 10-15 minutes in a CT scanner; no IV contrast needed for lung evaluation; patient lies still and breathes on command. Results reviewed by a radiologist within hours. Miliary CT pattern: random distribution of 1-2 mm nodules in all three lung zones bilaterally.

diagnosticTests.test: Sputum smear and mycobacterial culture (3 samples)
diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies acid-fast bacilli on smear (30-50% sensitivity in miliary TB) and grows Mycobacterium tuberculosis on culture for definitive diagnosis and drug susceptibility testing
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Three early-morning sputum samples collected on consecutive days. Smear results in 1-2 hours; culture requires 2-8 weeks on liquid (MGIT) or solid (Löwenstein-Jensen) media. Drug susceptibility results at 4-6 weeks from culture.

diagnosticTests.test: Xpert MTB/RIF nucleic acid amplification (sputum, BAL, CSF)
diagnosticTests.purpose: Detects M. tuberculosis DNA and rifampicin resistance within 2 hours; WHO-recommended first-line test
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Sputum or bronchoscopy sample processed in a cartridge-based automated system. Result in under 2 hours. Sensitivity 80-90% on sputum in smear-positive miliary TB; lower in HIV-co-infected patients.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Highest-yield single biopsy in cryptic miliary TB or when sputum is unavailable; detects granulomas histologically and grows mycobacteria on culture
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Performed under local anesthetic at the posterior iliac crest; 20-30 minutes. A trephine core and aspirate are sent for histology and mycobacterial culture. Histology result within 24-48 hours; culture within weeks.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Non-invasive confirmation of dissemination; choroidal tubercles are virtually pathognomonic of miliary TB when found with the clinical syndrome
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: An ophthalmologist dilates the pupil and examines the retina with an indirect ophthalmoscope; 10-15 minutes. Choroidal tubercles appear as 1-3 mm pale, indistinct yellow-white lesions near the optic disc.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Detects mycobacteremia; most useful in HIV-co-infected patients where bacteremia is more prominent
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: 20 mL blood collected into specialized culture bottles; incubated for up to 42 days. Positive yield 20-50% in HIV miliary TB. Results flagged automatically by continuous monitoring systems.

treatmentOverview: Miliary tuberculosis is treated with the same standard four-drug anti-TB regimen used for pulmonary TB, but the intensive phase is often extended in clinical practice given the severity of dissemination. The WHO 2022 consolidated TB guidelines and NICE NG33 recommend: isoniazid (H) 5 mg/kg/day, rifampicin (R) 10 mg/kg/day, pyrazinamide (Z) 25 mg/kg/day, and ethambutol (E) 15 mg/kg/day for 2 months, followed by isoniazid and rifampicin for 4 months (2HRZE/4HR, total 6 months). For miliary TB with meningitis (TB meningitis), the total duration is extended to a minimum of 12 months, with the continuation phase lasting 10 months. Adjunct corticosteroids — dexamethasone 0.4 mg/kg/day for 4 weeks tapering over 8 weeks — are mandatory when meningitis or pericarditis coexist with miliary TB; their benefit in miliary TB without meningitis is not established but they are used in critically ill patients with diffuse bilateral pneumonia and respiratory failure at some centers. Pyridoxine 25-50 mg/day is co-prescribed with isoniazid to prevent peripheral neuropathy. All patients require baseline and monitoring of liver function tests (hepatotoxicity risk from H, R, and Z), visual acuity and color vision (ethambutol optic neuritis), and uric acid (pyrazinamide hyperuricemia). In HIV-co-infected miliary TB, antiretroviral therapy (ART) should be started within 2 weeks of commencing anti-TB therapy if CD4 count is below 50 cells/µL, or within 8 weeks for CD4 above 50 cells/µL, per WHO 2022 guidance. Immune reconstitution inflammatory syndrome (IRIS) from ART initiation can cause paradoxical worsening in up to 30% of cases; corticosteroids treat IRIS. Drug-resistant miliary TB (MDR-TB or XDR-TB) requires individualized regimens guided by drug susceptibility testing, preferably containing bedaquiline and linezolid per WHO 2022 guidelines. Nutritional support throughout treatment is essential; WHO recommends micronutrient supplementation for all TB patients.

medicalTreatments.name: Isoniazid 5 mg/kg/day (max 300 mg) + pyridoxine 25-50 mg/day
medicalTreatments.description: Bactericidal first-line drug for all forms of TB. Kills rapidly dividing bacilli; pyridoxine co-administration prevents isoniazid-induced peripheral neuropathy. Administered throughout the full 6-12 month course.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Central to the regimen achieving sputum conversion in over 80% by 2 months and cure in 95%+ of drug-susceptible TB with full 6-month treatment

medicalTreatments.name: Rifampicin 10 mg/kg/day (max 600 mg)
medicalTreatments.description: The most potent bactericidal anti-TB drug, active against both rapidly and slowly dividing bacilli. Strong CYP3A4 inducer — substantially reduces drug levels of antiretrovirals, oral contraceptives, and many other drugs. Red-orange discoloration of urine, tears, and sweat is expected and harmless.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Essential for shortening treatment duration to 6 months; rifampicin resistance (detected by Xpert MTB/RIF) is the most important driver of treatment failure

medicalTreatments.name: Pyrazinamide 25 mg/kg/day (max 2 g)
medicalTreatments.description: Bactericidal active against semi-dormant bacilli in acidic macrophage vacuoles; essential in the 2-month intensive phase. Main adverse effects: hepatotoxicity (monitor LFTs monthly) and hyperuricemia. Discontinue if ALT rises above 3× ULN with symptoms.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Responsible for shortening TB treatment from 12 months to 6 months in combination regimens; critical component of miliary TB treatment

medicalTreatments.name: Ethambutol 15-20 mg/kg/day
medicalTreatments.description: Bacteriostatic companion drug protecting against resistance emergence; inhibits arabinosyl transferase in mycobacterial cell wall synthesis. Monitor visual acuity and color discrimination monthly — optic neuritis is dose- and duration-dependent. Discontinue if visual changes occur.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Prevents emergence of isoniazid and rifampicin resistance when added to HRZE regimen; stopped at 2 months in drug-susceptible TB

medicalTreatments.name: Dexamethasone 0.4 mg/kg/day IV or oral, 4-week taper over 8 weeks
medicalTreatments.description: Mandatory adjunct when miliary TB involves the meninges (TB meningitis) or pericardium (TB pericarditis). Reduces meningeal inflammation, limits fibrotic sequelae, and improves neurological outcomes. May also be used in severe miliary pulmonary disease with respiratory failure.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Cochrane 2016 meta-analysis: dexamethasone reduces death and severe neurological disability in TB meningitis by approximately 30%; mandatory in WHO and NICE guidelines for TB meningitis

medicalTreatments.name: Bedaquiline + linezolid-based regimen (for MDR-TB)
medicalTreatments.description: WHO-recommended oral regimen for drug-resistant miliary TB; replaces toxic injectables (kanamycin, capreomycin). Bedaquiline inhibits mycobacterial ATP synthase; linezolid inhibits protein synthesis. 18-24 month course for MDR-TB.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: WHO 2022 consolidated guidelines: BPaLM regimen (bedaquiline, pretomanid, linezolid, moxifloxacin) achieves 89% treatment success in MDR-TB in TB-PRACTECAL trial; major advance over older MDR regimens

surgicalOptions.name: Bronchoscopy with bronchoalveolar lavage (BAL)
surgicalOptions.description: Diagnostic procedure to obtain microbiological samples when sputum is negative or inadequate; yields M. tuberculosis in 70-90% of smear-negative miliary TB. Trans-bronchial biopsy can be performed simultaneously for histology.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Diagnostic yield 70-90% for miliary TB; procedure morbidity very low; airway management precautions required for hypoxic patients

alternativeTreatments.name: Nutritional supplementation (micronutrients including zinc, vitamin D, and vitamin A)
alternativeTreatments.description: WHO recommends multi-micronutrient supplementation for all TB patients; low vitamin D is associated with increased TB susceptibility and micronutrient repletion in deficient patients modestly improves treatment outcomes in randomized trials from South Asia.

whySeeSpecialist: An infectious disease or respiratory medicine specialist with TB experience should manage miliary TB given the diagnostic complexity, drug toxicity monitoring requirements, and public health notification obligations. HIV co-infected patients require coordinated management with an HIV specialist to sequence ART and anti-TB therapy and manage IRIS.
doctorSelectionGuide: Choose a clinician with TB management experience and access to a WHO-accredited TB laboratory, chest imaging with CT, and bronchoscopy capability. Drug-resistant miliary TB should be referred to a national MDR-TB referral center with access to bedaquiline and linezolid. Meningeal involvement requires concurrent neurology or neurointensive care.
hospitalCriteria: Respiratory failure from diffuse miliary lung infiltrates requiring supplemental oxygen or mechanical ventilation
Concurrent TB meningitis with neurological signs — requires dexamethasone and close neuro-monitoring
Severe pancytopenia with active bleeding requiring platelet transfusion
Inability to take oral medications due to vomiting or altered consciousness
MDR or XDR miliary TB requiring specialized drug regimen initiation under supervision
keyFacilities: Tertiary hospitals with mycobacteriology laboratories and DST capability
Infectious disease or respiratory TB units with isolation facilities
MDR-TB national referral centers with access to bedaquiline and linezolid
ICU with respiratory support for severe miliary TB with respiratory failure
preventionStrategies: BCG vaccination at birth reduces miliary and meningeal TB risk in children by 70-80% — the most impactful preventive measure in high-burden settings.

Screen for and treat latent TB infection (LTBI) with isoniazid preventive therapy (6 months) or rifapentine-isoniazid (3 months, 12 weekly doses) before starting anti-TNF therapy, organ transplant, or other immunosuppression.
Initiate and maintain antiretroviral therapy in HIV-infected individuals to restore CD4 counts above 200 cells/µL and sharply reduce miliary TB risk.
Conduct TB contact tracing for all close contacts (household, workplace) of any miliary TB index case; offer LTBI testing and treatment to contacts with positive IGRA or TST.
Improve nutrition and address protein-energy malnutrition in TB-endemic communities — nutritional interventions alongside TB control reduce population-level TB incidence.
Ensure adequate ventilation in crowded living and working spaces to reduce airborne M. tuberculosis transmission that seeds latent infection pools.
lifestyleModifications: Complete the full prescribed anti-TB drug course without interruption; premature discontinuation leads to relapse, treatment failure, and drug resistance.
Abstain completely from alcohol throughout treatment — alcohol potentiates isoniazid and rifampicin hepatotoxicity and increases noncompliance.
Take isoniazid and pyrazinamide with food to reduce gastrointestinal side effects; rifampicin is better absorbed on an empty stomach but may be taken with food if nausea is severe.
Attend all monthly monitoring appointments for liver function tests, visual acuity (ethambutol), and clinical response assessment.
Report any yellow skin or eyes, dark urine, visual changes, or tingling/numbness in hands or feet immediately — these signs of drug toxicity require prompt dose adjustment or drug substitution.
Wear a surgical mask and ensure adequate indoor ventilation during the first 2 weeks of treatment when sputum smear may still be positive; observe standard respiratory isolation precautions.

recommended: High-protein diet (at least 1.2-1.5 g/kg/day) from lean meat, fish, eggs, legumes, and dairy — TB is a catabolic disease causing significant muscle wasting; protein repletion is essential
Zinc-rich foods (shellfish, meat, seeds, legumes) — zinc deficiency impairs macrophage function and cellular immunity essential for mycobacterial control
Vitamin D-rich foods or supplementation (800-2,000 IU/day) — low vitamin D is independently associated with active TB progression; supplementation improves immune activation in deficient patients
Foods rich in vitamin A and C (orange and yellow vegetables, citrus) to support mucosal immunity and antioxidant defenses
avoid: All alcohol throughout treatment — synergistically hepatotoxic with isoniazid, rifampicin, and pyrazinamide
Aged cheeses, pickled fish, and tyramine-rich foods in the rare cases where linezolid is used — linezolid has mild MAO-inhibitory activity and tyramine interaction risk
Excessive herbal supplements especially kava, comfrey, or chaparral which have independent hepatotoxicity

exerciseGuidelines: Complete bedrest is required during the acute febrile phase with respiratory compromise. Light physical activity is encouraged once fever resolves and respiratory status improves, typically after 2-4 weeks of treatment. Progressive walking and low-intensity exercise are supported throughout the treatment period to prevent muscle wasting and improve mood. Heavy manual labor and vigorous aerobic exercise should be deferred until completion of the intensive phase (2 months) and confirmed clinical response.
dailyManagement: Take all prescribed anti-TB drugs daily at the same time to maintain consistent drug levels; use a pill organizer or phone alarm to avoid missed doses.
Monitor urine color: rifampicin reliably turns urine orange-red; absence of discoloration suggests the dose was not absorbed or taken.
Check skin and eyes weekly for jaundice — drug-induced hepatitis can occur at any time during treatment and requires immediate medical evaluation.
Avoid antihistamines, cold remedies, and any new supplements or medications without pharmacist review — rifampicin interacts with hundreds of drugs via CYP3A4 induction.
Maintain a symptom diary recording fever, cough, appetite, and weight weekly and bring it to monthly clinic appointments.
prognosis: Miliary tuberculosis prognosis is determined by the extent of dissemination, the host immune status, and the speed of diagnosis and treatment. With prompt four-drug therapy in immunocompetent adults, overall treatment success rates are 75-85%, with case fatality of 10-20% in treated patients. HIV-co-infected miliary TB carries higher mortality: 25-35% in the first 2 months even with optimized therapy, related to immune reconstitution inflammatory syndrome, opportunistic co-infections, and delayed ART initiation. Cryptic miliary TB has particularly high mortality (50-80%) because of diagnostic delay. Miliary TB with meningitis adds neurological sequelae risk — hearing loss, cognitive impairment, cranial nerve palsies — in 15-30% of survivors even with corticosteroids. Children treated early for miliary TB have better outcomes than adults, with treatment success above 90% in drug-susceptible disease. Drug-resistant miliary TB (MDR-TB) carries treatment success rates of 60-70% with optimized bedaquiline-based regimens. Without treatment, miliary tuberculosis is fatal in virtually all cases.
recoveryTimeline: Fever typically resolves within 2-4 weeks of starting four-drug therapy. Miliary nodules on chest imaging resolve over 3-6 months. Sputum smear conversion occurs by 2 months in most drug-susceptible cases. Full 6-month treatment course mandatory even after clinical improvement. Neurological deficits from TB meningitis improve over 3-6 months but may be permanent in severe cases.
complications: Respiratory failure from diffuse miliary lung infiltrates — occurs in severe cases; requires ICU, supplemental oxygen, and sometimes mechanical ventilation; mortality above 40% in ventilated miliary TB.
TB meningitis developing in 20-30% of miliary TB — carries 30% mortality and 15-30% neurological sequelae (hearing loss, cognitive deficit, cranial nerve palsy) even with treatment.
Drug-induced hepatotoxicity from isoniazid, rifampicin, and pyrazinamide — monitor LFTs monthly; severe hepatitis requires regimen modification.
Immune reconstitution inflammatory syndrome (IRIS) when ART is started in HIV-co-infected patients — paradoxical clinical worsening; manage with corticosteroids.
Adrenal insufficiency from bilateral adrenal granulomatous destruction — presents as refractory hypotension and hyponatremia; requires lifelong hydrocortisone replacement.
Drug resistance (MDR-TB, XDR-TB) emerging from inadequate or interrupted treatment — requires 18-24 month individualized regimen with higher toxicity and lower cure rates.

supportResources.name: WHO Global TB Programme
supportResources.description: Global TB surveillance, treatment guidelines, and access to essential medicines for drug-resistant TB.

supportResources.name: CDC Tuberculosis
supportResources.description: US TB guidelines, drug susceptibility testing resources, and contact investigation protocols.

supportResources.name: Stop TB Partnership
supportResources.description: Global health partnership supporting TB control, patient advocacy, and access to diagnostics and drugs in low-income countries.

confusedWithConditions.name: Pulmonary Tuberculosis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Pulmonary TB is typically a localized upper-lobe cavitary or consolidative disease without the bilateral diffuse 1-2 mm miliary nodular pattern of miliary TB. Pulmonary TB patients are usually ambulatory with productive cough and night sweats over weeks; miliary TB presents with rapid high fever, dyspnea, hepatosplenomegaly, and systemic toxicity indicating multi-organ seeding.

confusedWithConditions.name: HIV/AIDS-associated opportunistic infections
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Advanced HIV causes PCP, MAC dissemination, and cryptococcal meningitis that can mimic miliary TB with bilateral chest infiltrates and constitutional symptoms. Distinguished by Pneumocystis jirovecii PCR on BAL, blood mycobacterial culture, serum cryptococcal antigen, and by the absence of the classic miliary nodular chest CT pattern in PCP (which shows ground-glass opacification, not discrete nodules).

confusedWithConditions.name: Schistosomiasis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Pulmonary schistosomiasis in the Katayama phase causes bilateral infiltrates, eosinophilia, and fever resembling miliary TB in returning travelers. Distinguished by marked peripheral eosinophilia (unusual in miliary TB), absence of acid-fast bacilli, and positive schistosome serology or stool/urine microscopy. Treated with praziquantel, not anti-TB drugs.

relatedConditions.name: Pulmonary Tuberculosis
relatedConditions.relevance: The most common form of TB; miliary TB represents the disseminated extreme of the same pathogen and shares the same first-line RIPE treatment regimen.

relatedConditions.name: HIV/AIDS
relatedConditions.relevance: HIV is the single largest risk factor for miliary TB globally; co-management of both infections is essential and requires careful timing of antiretroviral and anti-TB therapy.

relatedConditions.name: Acute Viral Hepatitis
relatedConditions.relevance: Drug-induced hepatotoxicity from isoniazid, rifampicin, and pyrazinamide must be distinguished from viral hepatitis when transaminases rise during TB treatment; baseline viral hepatitis serology is recommended before starting anti-TB therapy.

faqs.question: What is miliary tuberculosis?
faqs.answer: Miliary tuberculosis is a severe form of tuberculosis where Mycobacterium tuberculosis spreads through the bloodstream and seeds multiple organs simultaneously. The name comes from the millet-seed appearance of the tiny 1-2 mm granulomas on chest X-ray. It is the most dangerous form of TB and requires at least 6 months of four-drug antibiotic therapy.

faqs.question: What does miliary tuberculosis look like on a chest X-ray?
faqs.answer: Miliary TB produces a bilateral pattern of countless uniformly sized 1-2 mm nodules distributed throughout all lung zones on chest X-ray, resembling scattered millet seeds. High-resolution CT detects this pattern 1-2 weeks earlier than plain X-ray and better defines the random distribution and size uniformity that distinguish miliary TB from other lung conditions.

faqs.question: Who is most at risk for miliary tuberculosis?
faqs.answer: HIV-infected individuals with low CD4 counts, children under 5 without BCG vaccination, patients on anti-TNF drugs or organ-transplant immunosuppression, and severely malnourished individuals face the highest risk. These conditions impair the granuloma formation that normally contains TB bacteria, allowing bloodstream spread to occur.

faqs.question: How is miliary tuberculosis diagnosed?
faqs.answer: Diagnosis combines chest CT (miliary nodule pattern), sputum smear and culture, Xpert MTB/RIF test, and fundoscopy for choroidal tubercles (pathognomonic of dissemination). Bone marrow biopsy gives the highest yield in cases where sputum is negative. Blood cultures for mycobacteria are useful in HIV-infected patients with bacteremia.

faqs.question: How is miliary tuberculosis treated?
faqs.answer: Standard 4-drug anti-TB therapy: isoniazid, rifampicin, pyrazinamide, and ethambutol for 2 months, then isoniazid and rifampicin for 4 more months (total 6 months). If meningitis coexists, treatment extends to 12 months and dexamethasone is added. Drug-susceptible miliary TB achieves treatment success in 75-85% of cases.

faqs.question: What are the symptoms of miliary tuberculosis?
faqs.answer: The main symptoms are prolonged high fever lasting weeks, severe fatigue, weight loss, night sweats, and dry cough with breathlessness. Physical examination typically reveals enlarged liver and spleen. Retinal examination may show choroidal tubercles. If the meninges are involved, headache and neck stiffness develop.

faqs.question: Is miliary tuberculosis contagious?
faqs.answer: Miliary TB can be contagious if sputum smear is positive, as droplets containing M. tuberculosis spread through the air. However, many miliary TB patients have low sputum bacillary loads. Standard respiratory isolation (negative-pressure room or surgical mask) should be maintained until two sputum smears on consecutive days are negative.

faqs.question: What is the survival rate for miliary tuberculosis?
faqs.answer: With prompt four-drug anti-TB therapy, case fatality is approximately 10-20% in immunocompetent adults. HIV-co-infected patients have 25-35% mortality in the first two months. Cryptic miliary TB carries 50-80% mortality due to diagnostic delay. Untreated miliary TB is fatal in virtually all cases.

faqs.question: What are choroidal tubercles?
faqs.answer: Choroidal tubercles are 1-3 mm pale yellow-white granulomas visible on fundoscopic examination of the retina in 10-30% of miliary TB patients. Their presence in the appropriate clinical context is virtually diagnostic of miliary TB and avoids the need for invasive biopsy. All suspected miliary TB patients should have fundoscopic examination.

faqs.question: Does miliary tuberculosis affect the brain?
faqs.answer: Yes, in 20-30% of cases. Hematogenous seeding of the meninges causes TB meningitis with headache, neck stiffness, and neurological deterioration. TB meningitis requires extension of treatment to 12 months and addition of dexamethasone corticosteroid. Even with treatment, 15-30% of TB meningitis survivors have permanent neurological sequelae.

faqs.question: Can miliary tuberculosis be prevented?
faqs.answer: BCG vaccination in infancy reduces miliary and meningeal TB by 70-80% in children. Isoniazid preventive therapy for people with latent TB who are about to start anti-TNF drugs or organ transplant substantially reduces reactivation risk. HIV treatment maintaining CD4 counts above 200 cells/µL significantly reduces miliary TB probability.

faqs.question: How long does miliary tuberculosis treatment take?
faqs.answer: Standard drug-susceptible miliary TB requires 6 months of daily antibiotic therapy. When TB meningitis coexists, treatment extends to 12 months. MDR-TB miliary disease requires 18-24 months with a different drug combination. Treatment must be completed even if symptoms resolve in the first weeks to prevent relapse and resistance.

faqs.question: What is the difference between miliary and pulmonary tuberculosis?
faqs.answer: Pulmonary TB is a localized lung infection, typically in the upper lobes with cavities or consolidation. Miliary TB is disseminated disease with seeding of multiple organs via the bloodstream, producing the bilateral 1-2 mm nodule pattern on CT. Miliary TB causes more severe systemic illness and higher mortality than localized pulmonary TB.

faqs.question: Can you get miliary tuberculosis more than once?
faqs.answer: Yes, particularly if the underlying immune deficiency is not corrected. HIV-infected patients on antiretroviral therapy with sustained viral suppression and CD4 recovery are at substantially lower risk of recurrence. Drug-resistant miliary TB can emerge from inadequate initial treatment and represents a distinct second episode requiring a different drug regimen.

faqs.question: What blood tests are abnormal in miliary tuberculosis?
faqs.answer: Elevated ESR and CRP reflect systemic inflammation. Pancytopenia (low hemoglobin, platelets, and white cells) from bone marrow involvement occurs in up to 50% of cases. Hyponatremia is seen with adrenal involvement. Elevated liver enzymes (ALP and ALT) reflect hepatic granulomas. IGRA or TST may be positive but are negative in 30-50% of miliary TB.
